summary:
The Battle of Gaugamela (331 BC) was the decisive engagement of [[event:75|Alexander the Great's Empire]]'s conquest of the Persian Achaemenid Empire, fought on a flat plain near modern Mosul in Iraq that Darius III had specially prepared by clearing away obstacles to give his war chariots room to operate. Despite being heavily outnumbered, Alexander's forces shattered the Persian army in a battle of brilliant tactical improvisation, ending Achaemenid power and making Alexander master of the Persian Empire.

Darius had assembled an enormous force representing the full resources of his empire — including war elephants, scythed chariots, and levies from Bactria, Scythia, and India — and chose a site suited to his numbers. Alexander's plan was to pin the Persian center while creating a gap through which his Companion cavalry could charge directly at Darius. During the battle, a gap opened in the Macedonian line as Alexander pursued success on the right flank while his left was under pressure; Alexander turned his cavalry and struck the Persian center, aiming directly for Darius. As at [[event:1032|Battle of Issus]], Darius fled when he saw Alexander's cavalry crashing toward him, causing the Persian army to dissolve in panic.

Alexander entered Babylon, Susa, and Persepolis in triumph, seizing the accumulated treasure of the Persian empire — enough wealth to fundamentally change the economics of the Greek world. He burned Persepolis, reportedly at the urging of the Athenian courtesan Thais, in symbolic revenge for the Persian burning of Athens in 480 BC during the [[event:1001|Greco-Persian Wars]]. With Darius dead (murdered by his own satrap Bessus shortly after), Alexander declared himself Great King and began the administrative challenge of governing an empire that dwarfed anything a Greek had previously imagined ruling.
